schema_json.description:
LLM contract for the agentic search loop 'Reason' step.

After each round of searches, the LLM analyzes results and decides
whether to continue searching or converge. Generates targeted
follow-up queries informed by what was found so far.

key_findings.description: 3-5 key findings from the latest round
key_findings.title: Key Findings

perspective_gaps.description: Underrepresented STORM perspectives in current results
perspective_gaps.title: Perspective Gaps

web_queries.description: 3-6 web queries for the next round
web_queries.title: Web Queries

academic_queries.description: 1-3 Semantic Scholar queries using precise terminology
academic_queries.title: Academic Queries

exa_queries.description: 0-1 semantic queries for Exa neural search
exa_queries.title: Exa Queries

should_continue.description: Whether another search round is needed
should_continue.title: Should Continue

reasoning.description: Brief rationale for the convergence assessment
reasoning.title: Reasoning

knowledge_gaps.description: Specific knowledge gaps identified from reading content
knowledge_gaps.title: Knowledge Gaps

schema_json.title: AgenticRoundAnalysis

prompt:
You are a research search strategist conducting an agentic deep research loop.

RESEARCH QUESTION: What is the empirical evidence on the impact of minimum wage increases on small business employment in OECD countries since 2010?

ROUND HISTORY:
Round 1: 9 queries -> 76 web + 30 academic, 65 new URLs, 6 pages read

KNOWLEDGE ACQUIRED (6 pages read so far):
[Page 1] Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss
  Summary: Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum wage increases, largely driven by concerns about job losses. After a minimum wage increase, the story goes, many employers will not be able to afford to pay their workers the new higher minimum wage and will therefore shrink their payrolls. If these job losses are large enough, they could even swamp the higher wages and lead to lower overall wage income for the entire group of
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum wage increases, largely driven by concerns about job losses.; A new review that I co-authored with Arindrajit Dube finds that most minimum wage studies find no job losses or only small disemployment effects.; This conclusion is strengthened by focusing on the studies that examine broad groups of low-wage workers or the overall workforce, not just narrow segments like teenagers.
[Page 2] Research on the Effects of Raising the Minimum Wage
  Summary: Recent minimum wage increases in 20 states and efforts by states including New York and California, and cities such as Seattle, to raise the minimum wage to $15 per hour have ignited the debate over the labor market effects of raising the minimum wage. An effort to set a federal $15 minimum wage has gained new traction under the Biden administration. Researchers using different methods and control groups often come up with different findings.
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Recent minimum wage increases in 20 states and efforts by states including New York and California, and cities such as Seattle, to raise the minimum wage to $15 per hour have ignited the debate over the labor market effects of raising the minimum wage.; An effort to set a federal $15 minimum wage has gained new traction under the Biden administration.
[Page 3] What does the scholarly research say about whether raising the ...
  Summary: What does the scholarly research say about whether raising the minimum wage negatively impacts employment? Our Work We conducted a systematic literature review of peer-reviewed articles published in the U.S. between 2004 and 2019 that assess the effect of raising the minimum wage on employment. Our search returned 144 studies and, of those, we identified 50 that directly addressed our research question above.
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Our Work We conducted a systematic literature review of peer-reviewed articles published in the U.S. between 2004 and 2019 that assess the effect of raising the minimum wage on employment.; Eight (8) studies showed mixed or inconclusive results.; In recent empirical studies, minimum wage raises do not appear to substantially lower employment, with many studies reporting employment effects indistinguishable from zero or random noise. 2.
[Page 4] [PDF] Criticisms of Minimum Wage Increases Lag Behind Latest Research
  Summary: Instead of focusing on the employment effect on teens and workers at or below the minimum wage, the recent research looks at the impact of changes on a large share of affected low-wage workers. 2 The emerging consensus is that the low-wage workforce, as a whole, is better off after an increase in the minimum wage. The latest domestic and international evidence shows that higher wage floors discourage low-pay employment and encourage the creation of good jobs with higher wages, more security, and
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Instead of focusing on the employment effect on teens and workers at or below the minimum wage, the recent research looks at the impact of changes on a large share of affected low-wage workers. 2 The emerging consensus is that the low-wage workforce,; Critics of minimum wage increases mischaracterize the employment effects in the research Critics tend to focus on the possibility that a gradual increase in the minimum wage to $15 by 2025 could potentially cost about 1% of U.S. workers their jobs. 4; The authors also show that low-wage workers saw wage gains of approximately 7% after a minimum wage increase, yet little change in employment years later.

  Uncovered: Regulatory, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends

Based on what has been READ:
1. Decompose the research question into 5-8 sub-questions.
   Which sub-questions have ZERO or WEAK evidence?
   Which sub-questions are well-answered with strong evidence?
2. What CONTRADICTIONS exist between sources? Flag conflicting evidence.
3. What specific KNOWLEDGE GAPS remain? List them as specific factual questions
   we CANNOT yet answer. 'none' is almost never correct for a complex research question.
4. Rate overall confidence in accumulated knowledge: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH with reasoning.
5. Which perspectives need more coverage?
6. Generate 3-6 targeted WEB queries to fill those gaps.
7. Generate 1-3 ACADEMIC queries using precise terminology.
8. Optionally generate 0-1 semantic EXA queries.
9. Assess ANSWER COMPLETENESS for the research question:
   - List specific sub-questions that remain UNANSWERED.
   - For each answered sub-question, rate evidence strength (weak/moderate/strong).
   - 'expanding': Major sub-questions still unanswered.
   - 'narrowing': Most sub-questions answered, seeking confirmation/depth.
   - 'saturated': All sub-questions answered with strong evidence.
10. Should we continue searching?

Focus on queries that address content-level gaps, not just missing URLs.
